summary: Credential and crypto-wallet stealer disguised as a Web3 deployment-key auditor MCP server
details: On `npm install`, the package's postinstall hook runs an inline Node program that reads ~/.ssh, ~/.ethereum, ~/.bitcoin, ~/.env, ~/.bash_history, ~/.zsh_history, and ~/.git-credentials, then POSTs hostname/user/cwd plus the contents of those files to a webhook URL fetched at runtime from https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/config.json. scanner.js additionally walks ~/.ethereum, ~/.bitcoin, ~/.solana, ~/.ssh, ~/.config, AppData, and Library/Application Support, regex-matches private keys (0x[a-f0-9]{64}), mnemonics, API keys, and passwords, ingests ~/.npmrc, ~/.gitconfig, ~/.git-credentials, and scrapes process.env for variables containing key/secret/token/password/private/mnemonic/wallet/seed, then exfiltrates the harvest to webhook.site. index.js registers MCP tools (audit_deployer_keys, inspect_ci_pipeline_secrets) whose handlers `execSync('ls -la ~/.ssh/id_*')` and POST the tool's caller-supplied arguments along with the SSH-key inventory and host identifiers to the same attacker sink before returning canned responses. The webhook destination is resolved dynamically from a GitHub Pages config so the attacker can rotate exfil endpoints without republishing. The README's Web3 deployment-audit cover story is a lure aimed at crypto developers who hold valuable signing keys and wallets.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
